metadata.split_role: Core/restored baseline mushroom records kept at the original path.
metadata.notes: Core/restored mushroom records retained at the original mushrooms.json path. Expanded mushroom records now load from smaller grouped files listed in split_manifest. v4.2.80 adds a core mushroom credibility pass: source links, use roles, foraging values, card notes, and cleaned placeholder use text for high-visibility mushrooms. v4.2.99-r2026-05-11-fold-mushroom-photo-patches1: folded mushroom photo patch records directly into canonical source records and retired runtime PHOTO_PATCH_PATHS. v4.3.0-r2026-05-11-data-chunk-size-cleanup1 split active data sources by size; record bodies and source order preserved.

split_manifest.role: Core/restored baseline mushroom records kept at the original path.
Expanded porcini, Tylopilus, dark-pored, and brown bolete records.
Expanded red/yellow, blue-staining, red-pored, and caution-lane bolete records.
Expanded Suillus and Leccinum/scaber-stalk bolete records.
Expanded long-stemmed, peppery, gilled-bolete, wood-inhabiting, and oddball bolete records.

review_reason_audit.rule: Normalize explicit review signals into review_reasons/review_note and prefix notes for Needs Review list visibility.

metadata.split_note: v4.3.0-r2026-05-11-data-chunk-size-cleanup1 split this active data source by file size to keep working chunks under roughly 75–100 KB. Record bodies and source order were preserved.

records.notes: Hard woody bracket fungus with a white pore surface that bruises brown and can be scratched for drawing.
records.short_reason: Original app species restored into the standalone modular build.
records.medicinal_uses: Traditional/reishi-adjacent interest exists in some sources, but this app treats it mainly as a non-food identification and practical-use species.
records.culinary_uses: Not a food mushroom; woody, perennial bracket.
records.edibility_detail: Avoid for food in this guide; keep as an identification/caution record.
records.general_notes: Best treated as a shelf fungus ID entry rather than a food species.
records.foraging_value: Other use — excellent craft/tinder curiosity; not food
records.other_uses: Classic artist's conk: the fresh white pore surface bruises brown and can be scratched to make durable drawings. Hard dried brackets are also useful as teaching/display specimens.
records.field_identification: Hard woody shelf on hardwoods; dull zoned upper surface; white pore surface stains brown where scratched.
records.mushroom_card_note: Useful as an artist's conk, not as food: scratch the white pore surface to make brown drawings.

images_structured.title: Artist's conk pore surface / drawing surface

image_review_reasons: Commons-backed field photo patch appended in v4.2.60-r2026-04-28-mushroom-photo-patch6.

records.short_reason: Original app species restored into the standalone modular build.
records.culinary_uses: Edible but not usually ranked with choice boletes; use only young, firm specimens after confident ID near ash.
records.edibility_detail: A recognizable ash-associated bolete. Quality is modest; older specimens soften quickly.

mushroom_profile.summary: Minimal bolete seed entry for Ash-tree Bolete. Added in the Michigan / Upper Midwest bolete pass; fuller ID notes, sources, and images still need review.
staining: Pores bruise brownish to olive; flesh may show blue-green tones
mushroom_profile.season_note: June through October; summer and fall near ash.
research_notes: Species seed added from the Michigan / Upper Midwest bolete pass.
Needs source links and fuller field notes.

records.field_identification: Grows on the ground near ash trees. Cap is yellowish-brown to reddish-brown and often wavy or irregular; stem is often off-center; pores are large, shallow, angular, radially arranged, and can bruise olive to brownish or sometimes bluish.
records.edibility_notes: Edible but not usually ranked with choice boletes; use only young, firm specimens after confident ID near ash.
curation_notes: Original app species restored into the standalone modular build.

Minimal species seed. Added during the bolete expansion pass for the clean app; details, seasonality, and images still need a later review. Baseline audit pass added lane, seasonality, commonness, food quality, host filtering, and comparison notes.
This record still needs species-level images and source links, but the core field-guide traits are now scaffolded for sorting and filtering.

edible_use.notes: Edible but not usually ranked with choice boletes; use only young, firm specimens after confident ID near ash.

images_structured.title: Ash-tree bolete cap and pore surface

image_review_reasons: Commons-backed field photo patch appended in v4.2.57-r2026-04-28-mushroom-photo-patch5.

records.notes: Shelf-forming oyster on aspen and related hardwoods.
records.short_reason: Original app species restored into the standalone modular build.
records.culinary_uses: Cooked like other oyster mushrooms: sauté, add to soups, or use in mixed mushroom dishes. Best when young and fresh before the margins dry out.
records.general_notes: Treat as a gilled wood-rotting oyster rather than 'other'. In the U.P. this is a late-season hardwood oyster-type entry and should be compared with pearl and summer oysters.

mushroom_profile.season_note: Late summer into fall on dead aspen and related hardwoods.

records.field_identification: Shelf-like oyster mushroom on aspen or other Populus wood, with decurrent white gills and little to no central stem.
records.mushroom_card_note: A good edible oyster on aspen/poplar wood; best young and cooked.
image_review_reasons: Exact-slug photo batch-pack record appended in v4.2.64-r2026-04-28-mushroom-photo-batchpack3-exact (Batch A — gilled/toothed edible-style records); source URLs came from uploaded mushrooms.json.
records.photo_patch_basis: exact slug match in uploaded mushrooms.json

records.notes: Bay Bolete is treated here as Imleria badia. Older app data also used the scientific-name slug imleria-badia; that duplicate has been merged into this page.
records.short_reason: Original app species restored into the standalone modular build.
records.medicinal_uses: Original app included medicinal notes or related use context for this entry. Detailed copyback is still pending.
records.culinary_uses: Generally regarded as edible when confidently identified and cooked; avoid old, buggy, or waterlogged specimens.
records.edibility_detail: Use only after confirming the bay-brown cap, yellow pores that bruise grayish blue, conifer/decayed-wood setting, and non-reticulate wrinkled stem.

mushroom_profile.season_note: Summer and fall in northeastern North America and the upper Midwest.
mushroom_profile.summary: Minimal bolete seed entry for Bay Bolete. Added in the Michigan audit pass; fuller ID notes, sources, and images still need review.
research_notes: Species seed added from the Michigan bolete audit.
Needs source links and fuller field notes.

records.field_identification: Bay-brown to reddish-brown cap, pale yellow pores that bruise grayish blue, wrinkled non-reticulate stem, and frequent fruiting near conifers or very decayed mossy wood.

use_links.label: MushroomExpert: Imleria badia
use_links.notes: Conifer/decayed wood association, blue-gray bruising pores, and stem traits.

records.edibility_notes: Cooked food. Generally regarded as edible when confidently identified and cooked; avoid old, buggy, or waterlogged specimens.
curation_notes: Original app species restored into the standalone modular build.

Recreated locally from the original app baseline checklist for the modular standalone build. Baseline audit pass added lane, seasonality, commonness, food quality, host filtering, and comparison notes.
This record still needs species-level images and source links, but the core field-guide traits are now scaffolded for sorting and filtering.

edible_use.notes: Generally regarded as edible when confidently identified and cooked; avoid old, buggy, or waterlogged specimens.

records.alias_record_note: Hidden from lists as a duplicate/legacy alias of Imleria badia; retained so older look-alike links and searches do not break.
records.duplicate_note: Duplicate Bay Bolete record kept only as a legacy slug alias.

images_structured.title: Bay bolete cap and pore view
images_structured.part_or_stage: Bay bolete cap and pore view

image_review_reasons: v4.2.52-r2026-04-27-mushroom-photo-patch3-append1 appended 3 Commons-backed image(s) for list/detail/lightbox use.

records.notes: Cascading white tooth fungus with branched clusters rather than one round pom-pom mass.
records.short_reason: Original app species restored into the standalone modular build.
records.medicinal_uses: Hericium species have modern supplement interest, but this entry is treated primarily as a food mushroom.
records.culinary_uses: Choice edible when young and white. Cook like lion's mane or other Hericium: sauté, brown in butter, or use in rich mushroom dishes.
records.general_notes: One of the Hericium mushrooms; useful to distinguish from lion's mane and coral tooth.
records.edibility_detail: Choice edible when fresh and tender.

mushroom_profile.season_note: Late summer through fall on hardwood logs and trunks.

records.field_identification: Branched white tooth fungus with longer teeth clustered on branch tips; found on hardwood logs or wounds.
records.mushroom_card_note: Choice Hericium: branched white clusters with hanging teeth, usually on hardwood.

images_structured.title: Bear's head tooth supporting field view
Bear's head tooth additional field view

image_review_reasons: Exact-slug photo batch-pack record appended in v4.2.64-r2026-04-28-mushroom-photo-batchpack3-exact (Batch A — gilled/toothed edible-style records); source URLs came from uploaded mushrooms.json.
records.photo_patch_basis: exact slug match in uploaded mushrooms.json

records.notes: Look for pale hoof-like brackets on birch with a fine white pore surface underneath.
records.short_reason: Original app species restored into the standalone modular build.
records.medicinal_uses: Traditional tea/decoction and historical medicinal-use interest; use conservatively and do not treat as a cure.
records.culinary_uses: Not a table mushroom; corky, bitter, and tough.
records.edibility_detail: Generally treated as non-culinary; better known for utility and tea-type use than as food.
records.general_notes: A birch specialist and a good host-linked ID species for the app.

mushroom_profile.season_note: Fruiting bodies persist and can be found year-round on birch.

records.other_uses: Also known historically as razor-strop fungus and as an ember/tinder material.
records.field_identification: Whitish to tan annual bracket on birch, often with a rounded inrolled margin and sunken pore surface.
records.mushroom_card_note: A birch-only polypore with traditional tea/tinder/utility interest, not a frying-pan mushroom.

images_structured.title: Birch polypore on birch field view

image_review_reasons: Commons-backed field photo patch appended in v4.2.57-r2026-04-28-mushroom-photo-patch5.

records.notes: Bitter Bolete is treated here as the Tylopilus felleus concept: a bitter, pink-pored bolete often confused with porcini-type mushrooms. North American material may not all be true European T. felleus, so use this page as a field-guide concept rather than a final microscope-level taxonomy claim.
records.short_reason: Original app species restored into the standalone modular build.
records.culinary_uses: Niche edible with an intensely bitter flavor. Most foragers skip it because even a small amount can make a dish unpleasant, but it is retained in the edible mushroom list as an acquired-taste bittering mushroom rather than a poison/caution species.
records.edibility_detail: Use only if intentionally seeking strong bitterness; otherwise keep it separate from good edible boletes because a small piece can dominate a pan.
records.general_notes: Key field clues: pinkish pores as it matures, a strongly bitter nibble-and-spit taste, and dark netting on the stalk. Often confused with porcini/king bolete types.

mushroom_profile.season_note: Usually encountered from July through October in the Upper Midwest / Great Lakes window.
mushroom_profile.summary: Pink-pored Tylopilus with an intensely bitter taste. Not poisonous in the usual field-guide sense, but most foragers skip it because it can ruin a meal; kept here as a niche/acquired-taste edible.
research_notes: Species seed added from the Michigan / Upper Midwest bolete pass.
Needs source links and fuller field notes.

records.poisoning_effects: Large amounts may cause stomach upset in some people; the practical problem is intense bitterness and confusion with better edible porcini-type boletes.
records.edibility_notes: Inedible because of intense bitterness rather than dangerous poisoning. Keep it out of the Caution page unless a future record adds a real toxicity concern.
curation_notes: Original app species restored into the standalone modular build.

Recreated locally from the original app baseline checklist for the modular standalone build. Baseline audit pass added lane, seasonality, commonness, food quality, host filtering, and comparison notes.
This record still needs species-level images and source links, but the core field-guide traits are now scaffolded for sorting and filtering.

use_links.label: MushroomExpert: Tylopilus felleus
use_links.notes: Pink pore surface, bitter taste, conifer association, and Michigan taxonomic note.

records.duplicate_note: Duplicate Bitter Bolete record kept only as a legacy slug alias.
records.toxicity_notes: Not treated as a poison species in this guide; retained as a niche/acquired-taste edible with a strong bitter flavor.
image_review_reasons: v4.2.52-r2026-04-27-mushroom-photo-patch3-append1 appended 3 Commons-backed image(s) for list/detail/lightbox use.

records.notes: Spring morels with darker ridges and a fully attached cap; hollow from tip through stem.
records.short_reason: Original app species restored into the standalone modular build.
records.culinary_uses: Choice spring edible; always cook thoroughly. Slice lengthwise to confirm a hollow interior and to clean out grit or insects.
records.general_notes: Grouped here as a practical field-guide complex rather than a single strict species concept.
records.edibility_detail: Excellent edible; false morels are the main danger.

mushroom_profile.season_note: Primarily a spring mushroom, often during leaf-out and warming soil.

records.field_identification: True morel with pitted cap, hollow interior from cap through stem, and cap attached to the stem; darkening ridges in black morels.
records.mushroom_card_note: Prime spring mushroom; confirm hollow true-morel structure and cook thoroughly.
image_review_reasons: Commons-backed field photo batch-pack candidate appended in v4.2.62-r2026-04-28-mushroom-photo-batchpack2 (Batch A — morels and early morel relatives).
records.photo_patch_group: Batch A — morels and early morel relatives

records.notes: Dark hollow trumpet with no true gills; the outside fertile surface is smooth to wrinkled, not bladed.
records.short_reason: Original app species restored into the standalone modular build.
records.culinary_uses: Choice edible with deep, aromatic flavor. Excellent fresh or dried; use in sauces, soups, eggs, rice, or anywhere a concentrated mushroom flavor helps.
records.general_notes: Easy to miss because of its dark color, especially in leaf litter.
records.edibility_detail: Choice edible with a strong aroma when dried.

mushroom_profile.season_note: Most often late summer into fall in moist hardwood woods.

records.field_identification: Dark hollow trumpet or vase with no true gills; fertile outer surface smooth to shallowly wrinkled.
records.mushroom_card_note: Prime edible trumpet; easy to miss on the forest floor but excellent fresh or dried.
image_review_reasons: Commons-backed field photo batch-pack candidate appended in v4.2.61-r2026-04-28-mushroom-photo-batchpack1 (Batch B — common edible forms).
